pattern: Filling a grid where each cell holds the best subsequence length for two string prefixes captures the choice to match a character or skip one side.
hints: Think about the answer for prefixes: how does the best length for text1[..i] and text2[..j] relate to shorter prefixes?
When the current characters match they extend a previous diagonal answer by one; when they differ you take the better of dropping one character from either side.
Build a table dp[i][j] = longest common subsequence of the first i and first j characters; the final cell is the answer.

edge_inputs.description: repeated characters with a longer common run

stress.description: two random strings over a tiny alphabet to force overlaps
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n1 = rng.randint(1, max(1, size))
    n2 = rng.randint(1, max(1, size))
    a = ''.join(rng.choice('abc') for _ in range(n1))
    b = ''.join(rng.choice('abc') for _ in range(n2))
    return [a, b]
stress.note: small alphabet guarantees nontrivial common subsequences
